issue_title: az aks browse doesn't work when one of the pod is in pending state
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
I had deployed an application with 5 replicas all pods were running, and tried to access the 'az aks browse -g group -n name' the browser opened with kubernetes dashboard. But when i scaled the replicas to 100, due to a node capacity few pods were in pending state and this has caused connection error and the kubernestes dashboard crashed. Again tried to execute 'az aks browse.....' but got the below error

Proxy running on http://127.0.0.1:8001/
Press CTRL+C to close the tunnel...
error: unable to forward port because pod is not running. Current status=Pending

**To Reproduce**
1. Deploy app with 5 replicas and 2 nodes
2. Execute 'az aks browser -g <<resource_group>> -n <<clustername>> 
3. Ecale the app to 100 replicas
4. Few pods goes to pending state
5. Kubernetes cluster dashboard closes / crashes
6. Execute 'az aks browser -g <<resource_group>> -n <<clustername>> 
7. Dashboard doesnt open, instead the error message shown
Proxy running on http://127.0.0.1:8001/
Press CTRL+C to close the tunnel...
error: unable to forward port because pod is not running. Current status=Pending

**Expected behavior**
I expect the dashboard to open regardless of pod state, this is how gks behaves.  This needs to work in same fashion as gks. Otherwise,  its disturbing developer experience

issue_title: [Network] How to attach a NIC to a load balancer's inbound NAT pool?
issue_body:
Is there a sample somewhere of using the inbound NAT pool for a load balancer instance? I looked [here](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli-samples/blob/master/network/beta-load-balancer.md) and didn't see it (only NAT rules are illustrated). I'm probably missing something, but I haven't been able to figure out how to attach a NIC to a load balancer's inbound NAT pool, or even confirm if I'm thinking about how to use them correctly :)

For example, if I create the following NAT pool, which I hoped to use in order to expose a series of sequential frontend ports that are NAT'd to a single port in a backend address pool:

```shell
az network lb inbound-nat-pool create -g <group> \
  -n <name> \
  --lb-name <lbName> \
  --protocol tcp \
  --frontend-port-range-start 2200 \
  --frontend-port-range-end 2205 \
  --backend-port 22
```

If I try to reference this pool by name, using the either the `--lb-address-pools` or `--lb-inbound-nat-rule` parameter of the `az network nic create` command, it fails to find it. That makes sense I guess, since the former is looking for an address pool, and the later is looking for a NAT *rule*, not a NAT *pool*. However, the `az network nic create` command doesn't have a `--lb-nat-pool(s)` parameter, so I'm not sure how to actually configure the NAT pool.

Additionally, the `az network nic ip-config` command group includes commands for adding/removing a NIC to a address pool or NAT rule, but once again, none of these seem to support a NAT *pool*.

Finally, if I look in the Azure portal underneath the LB instance, I don't actually see any mention of NAT pools anywhere, and the NAT pool I created via the CLI doesn't show up under the `Inbound NAT Rules` blade, so I'm somewhat confused.

I would love any clarification on this. I could achieve what I'm doing above by creating six separate NAT rules, and referencing them via the `--lb-inbound-nat-rule` parameter of `az network nic create`, but I really liked the idea of creating a NAT pool and attaching NICs to it in order to reserve a front-end port. Then again, maybe I'm completely misinterpreting the use of a NAT pool. Thanks!

issue_title: CI Version check script can allow changes to be logged under the wrong version
issue_body:
Because there is approx. a week between code complete and the public release, there exists about a week gap where merged PRs could inadvertently log their changes under the already cut release instead of under a new version for the subsequent release and the CI would not catch this.

For example:

1. CLI is code-complete and build released to Cloud Shell
2. The next PR for network inadvertently puts its changes under the existing version that has already been released to Cloud Shell. The CI will not fail.
3. On the release day, the packages are published to PyPI. 
4. A new PR is created for network and the CI complains. Only then is a new version added to HISTORY, but the PR that was merged earlier (which should logged under the new version) will still be listed in the old version.

---

issue_title: AADTokenCredentials doesn't properly refresh the token
issue_body:
It looks like the ServiceClient can't properly refresh the token if given AADTokenCredentials.  It tries to do a refresh by passing in session to the `refresh_session` method and expects it to transform the existing session, but it looks like AADTokenCredentials will instead return a new session.

Caller:
https://github.com/Azure/msrest-for-python/blob/master/msrest/service_client.py#L351

Callee:
https://github.com/Azure/msrestazure-for-python/blob/master/msrestazure/azure_active_directory.py#L263

Since the method accepts a parameter it never raises a `TypeError` and the fallback method of getting a session returned will never happen unless the user tries the whole thing again.

I originally thought it was an Azure SDK issue https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/2779 but it looks like it belongs either in here or with https://github.com/Azure/msrestazure-for-python.

I think the issue might be with what is expected from the `refresh_session`, since the implementation in msrestazure could be ambiguous.

issue_title: Release 1.0.4 wheel still installs "futures" in Python 3
issue_body:
Even if this setup.py has a syntax to ignore installing "futures" on Py3 (#23 for reference), the wheel on PyPI still installs it anyway. It's the because the environment where this wheel was *built* is old (older than setuptools 36.2) and the option is ignored silently (old setuptools cannot know it's missing something...).
More detail in this excellent blog
https://hynek.me/articles/conditional-python-dependencies/

There is several possible fixes to this problem:
- Most simple: be sure the build environment is using latest pip/setuptools/wheel always.
- Build and deploy from Travis: https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/deployment/pypi/
- Change the syntax to something allowed in older setuptools. [Example](https://github.com/Azure/msrest-for-python/blob/12fb980c3290daa9c83bacc7e43c3f1455904b8c/setup.py#L55-L58).

It's possible to re-upload a 1.0.4 with the fixed wheel, but probably not worth the effort. But should be addressed with future releases.

issue_title: GDAL
issue_body:
I'm trying to figure out how to get the sources that the machine throws these pesky errors going again:

`GDAL gave Failure 1: IllegalArgumentException: Invalid number of points in LinearRing found 3 - must be 0 or >= 4`

Here's the responsible code for this: 

```
                try:
                    centroid = geom.Centroid()
                except RuntimeError as e:
                    if 'Invalid number of points in LinearRing found' not in str(e):
                        raise
```

The conditional is supposed to be checking for a certain type of error and short-circuit handling, but I don't think it's doing what it's supposed to.  If I change the above to:

```
                try:
                    centroid = geom.Centroid()
                except RuntimeError as e:
                    _L.info("actual error raised: '{0}'".format(e))
                    _L.info("actual error raised: '{0}'".format(str(e)))
                    if 'Invalid number of points in LinearRing found' not in str(e):
                        raise
```

to log the actual error, I see the following in the output (I output both raw and cast as a string):

```
   309     32449.1  ERROR: GDAL gave Failure 1: IllegalArgumentException: Invalid number of points in LinearRing found 3 - must be 0 or >= 4
   309     32449.3   INFO: actual error raised: ''
   309     32449.5   INFO: actual error raised: ''
```

This tells me 2 things:

1.  `gdal_error_handler` defined and registered on lines 30 and 41 are getting the error before the `except Runtime` block
2.  the error `e` is "disappearing" before the error message test condition is firing

At least I **think** this is what's going on.  I know very little about Python or the intent of the authors of what should be done in these cases but the [special conditional for invalid linear rings](https://github.com/openaddresses/machine/blob/master/openaddr/conform.py#L700-L701) isn't operating as expected.

issue_title: Add functions thats subtracts a field from another field
issue_body:
See openaddresses/openaddresses#2195

Here's a example data that's motivating this: 

```
{
  "addrnum": "400 E",
  "unitid": "E",
  "fulladdr": "400 E EMERY AVE"
}
```

In this case it's very difficult if not impossible to write a `regexp` function that would know the difference between a unit `E` and an `E` that's an address pre-directional.   That is, without awareness of other fields values, it's not possible to know if the street value shoud be `E EMERY AVE` or `EMERY AVE`.  The data motivating this also has a wide variety of unit formats:

* `[0-9]+`
* `[A-Z]`
* `[A-Z]-?[0-9]+`
* `[0-9]+[A-Z]`
* `BLDG [0-9]+`
* a variety of one-off values that can't be captured by regexes easily

These conform function would look something like:

```
{
  "number": {
    "function": "remove_postfix",
    "field": "addrnum",
    "field_to_remove": "unitid",
  },
  "street": {
    "function": "remove_prefix",
    "field": "fulladdr",
    "field_to_remove": "addrnum"    
  },
  "unit": "unitid"
}
```

The result of these functions would be:

```
{
  "number": "400",
  "street": "EMERY AVE",
  "unit": "E"
}
```

In a larger context, there are some sources that have `number` identified as a distinct field but the `street` value can only be derived using a `regexp`.  In these cases, the `regexp` function can be replaced with `remove_prefix`.

issue_title: Spring2Constraint gets the dynamics wrong!
issue_body:
Hi! 

First of all, thanks for an awesome physics engine! I've been using Bullet for my research and noticed strange behavior of the btGeneric6Dof**Spring2**Constraint, which seemed to ignore an important principle of physics. I investigated this with a simple horizontal pendulum simulation: one dof (rotation about vertical axis, spring enabled, no gravity). I did several simulations with different pendulum length (distance from pivot point to mass). To my surprise, the frequency of oscillation did not change, no matter the length of the pendulum -> this is dynamically incorrect! The frequency should be inversely correlated with pendulum length. 
I simulated the same thing with the "depricated" btGeneric6Dof**Spring**Constraint and all of a sudden, without making any other changes, I could see the correct behavior: the frequency increased with decreasing pendulum length!

![constraints_frequency_length](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/36479450/36342410-c2451dec-13c3-11e8-8657-b5d3ee1b7bb0.png)

If there are any parameters I don't know of that would make Spring2Constraint to behave correctly, I would love to know! Otherwise, it would be great if you could fix the issue in the Spring2Constraint since it effects the accuracy of the physics simulations significantly. To my knowledge, the issue only occurs when the spring is enabled - the frequency of a pendulum driven only by gravity changes with the length.

Thanks!

issue_title: Add support for multiprocessing.Queue
Add support for retrieving randomly selected port numbers
issue_body:
If you pass `0` as the port to `aiohttp.web.run_app()`, aiohttp will select any available port. However, it is not currently possible to determine *which* port was selected. It would be useful in some cases to be able to determine which port is being used.

A motivating example for this is [the traad Python refactoring server](https://github.com/abingham/traad/tree/aiohttp). This listens for HTTP requests to perform various kinds of refactoring operations. It is typically used via a text editor like Emacs. Users of traad don't normally care what port it's running on; they may not even know that they're running an HTTP server at all. They just expect Emacs to "do the right thing". As such, it doesn't make sense to ask them to specify a port for the server. 

The emacs client for traad starts the server, telling it to choose any available port. Once the server starts, it prints its actual port to stdout and the emacs client parses that. This is possible in the current bottle-based implementation of traad, but the experimental aiohttp version is a impractical because we can't determine the port that it selects.

For my purposes, it would be great if I could register an `on_startup` listener that could then fetch the port from the app. But right now there's no way to get the port.

FWIW, there was [some discussion of this on stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/44610441/how-to-determine-which-port-aiohttp-selects-when-given-port-0).

issue_body:
## Long story short
I described an issues in https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/1619 Later I update aiohttp for new version however the problem is still here.

"{ "doc": "some data"}\nX-Custom-Header1=0\r\nX-Custom-Header2=custom2\r\nX-Custom-Header3={}\r\n\r\n

## Expected behaviour
aiohttp loads json body and skips trailers. Parsing of the next response should not read any trailers from previous response.

issue_title: Pagination is broken
issue_body:
## Issue

Commit 42dbd833187ff396e4b0e443eaa5fd0d20b84b66 appears to have broken pagination. The reproducer below works fine using aiobotocore 0.6.0 (with aiohttp 3.0.9), but breaks with aiobotocore 0.7.0 (with aiohttp 3.1.3).

```python
from pprint import pprint

import aiobotocore


async def main():
    session = aiobotocore.get_session()

    async with session.create_client('s3') as s3:
        paginator = s3.get_paginator('list_objects')
        async for page in paginator.paginate(Bucket='some-bucket'):
            for item in page.get('Contents', []):
                pprint(item)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(main())
```

And the resulting traceback:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 50, in <module>
    loop.run_until_complete(main())
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/3.6.5/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/asyncio/base_events.py", line 468, in run_until_complete
    return future.result()
  File "test.py", line 42, in main
    for item in page.get('Contents', []):
AttributeError: 'coroutine' object has no attribute 'get'
sys:1: RuntimeWarning: coroutine 'AioPageIterator.next_page' was never awaited
```

## Root cause

The problem is in `AioPageIterator`'s `__anext__`:
https://github.com/aio-libs/aiobotocore/blob/42fc4d9765d84b8d16168b750296859b85ac698e/aiobotocore/paginate.py#L93-L97

Rather than awaiting `self.next_page()`, the coroutine is being returned directly. This is definitely wrong; changing the last line to `return await self.next_page()` fixes the issue (I'll submit a PR).

What I don't understand is how it was working before, and why simply switching from using the `@asyncio.coroutine` decorator to native `async def` functions exposed it.

issue_title: Drop support for `old_where`
issue_body:
1) 1024-bit roots have only gotten more dangerous since we started this deprecation process
2) OpenSSL 1.0.1 has had this patch backported, including to distros like Ubuntu
3) OpenSSL 1.0.1, 1.0.0, and 0.9.8 are now EOL by upstream

The combination of all these factors means that keeping it around is more risk than is necessary.

To do this deprecation I recommend modifying `old_where` to simply return `where()` and emitting a warning. It can then be removed after the appropriate period.

issue_title: Wrong focus when remove many cells

issue_body:
# Steps to reproduce

1. Open a notebook.
2. Select many cells (start with the first one). The sum of height of the selected cells must be greater than the height of the visible area.
3. Delete the cells.

# Expected behaviour

The focus is on the next cell of the last removed cell and that cell is on the visible area.

# Current behaviour

The focus is on the next cell of the last removed cell **but that cell is not on the visible area.**

# Environment

- Notebook server: 4.2.1-1b1e6f608d
- Web browser: Chromium Version 55.0.2883.87 (64-bit) **and** Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64; rv:50.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/50.0

# Extra

[Video showing the bug.](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/files/716521/video.zip)

issue_title: Using IPython and Python machinery to import Jupyter notebooks
issue_body:
I am not sure where this conversation should take place so I will start here.  I feel like their is place in IPython or Jupyter to support generically importable notebooks.

I have been using notebooks as modules for over a year and a half now.  I believe the resting state of the notebook is most important to consider when designing notebooks, the interactive notebooks state is fleeting on the aggregate.  Designing notebooks as modules imposes fairly natural conventions for designing notebooks that are __restart and run all__ able and literate documents.


I have tried a few implementations.

1. My first attempt used nbconvert to convert notebooks to python source; basically the `--script` flag disappeared.

    * In this experiment, cached versions of python code created some very confusing situations as the number of notebooks grow.  In other words, `reload` did not work. 
    * This method uses the [`post_save_hook`](https://gist.github.com/tonyfast/49dc9f9f87a3eb4b9b57cd69b3d84a40) to create python files that retain no mappings to their source notebooks.

2. The next attempt was [`importable`](https://github.com/tonyfast/importable/blob/master/importable.ipynb) which was influenced by the [Import Notebooks](http://jupyter-notebook.readthedocs.io/en/stable/examples/Notebook/Importing%20Notebooks.html) example.  

    - This experiment places the import finder in `sys.meta_path` which provides to limited search capabilities compared to `sys.path_hooks` which explores all of the `sys.paths`.  
    + This approach introduces the `InputTransformManager` making magics and other syntactic sugar work.
    + Notebooks reload which is important for interactive module development.
    - There is no line level debugging making it very difficult to interactively import code in a notebook.
    

__3__. The current implementation has the alias [`rites`](https://github.com/deathbeds/rites).  


```python
    if __name__ == '__main__':
        %reload_ext rites.rites
```

* `rites` uses the python, ipython, and nbconvert machinery.
* It inserts a finder into `sys.path_hooks` exposing any notebook as potential source code.  Making it possible to load arbitrary notebooks as modules like the [Python DataScience Handbook]().


```python
    if __name__ == '__main__':
        # Change the name so we can import the notebook without the annoying importlib stuff.
        !cp PythonDataScienceHandbook/notebooks/02.00-Introduction-to-NumPy.ipynb PythonDataScienceHandbook/notebooks/_02_00_Introduction_to_NumPy.ipynb
        import PythonDataScienceHandbook.notebooks._02_00_Introduction_to_NumPy as intro
        print(dir(intro))
```

    ['__builtins__', '__complete__', '__doc__', '__file__', '__loader__', '__name__', '__output__', '__package__', '__spec__', 'np', 'numpy']


* There is an overloaded `JSONDecoder` that tracks line numbers in a file to provide useful debugging statement.


```python
    if __name__=='__main__':
            import Untitled85
```


    ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

    AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)

    <ipython-input-26-7a5ff210c165> in <module>()
          1 if __name__=='__main__':
    ----> 2         import Untitled85
    

    ~/anaconda/envs/p6/lib/python3.6/importlib/_bootstrap.py in _find_and_load(name, import_)


    ~/anaconda/envs/p6/lib/python3.6/importlib/_bootstrap.py in _find_and_load_unlocked(name, import_)


    ~/anaconda/envs/p6/lib/python3.6/importlib/_bootstrap.py in _load_unlocked(spec)


    ~/rites/rites/rites.ipynb in exec_module(Module, module)
        233     "            from IPython.utils.capture import capture_output\n",
        234     "            with capture_output(stdout=False, stderr=False) as output:\n",
    --> 235     "                super(type(Module), Module).exec_module(module)\n",
        236     "                try: module.__complete__ = True\n",
        237     "                except BaseException as Exception: module.__complete__ = Exception\n",


    ~/rites/rites/rites.ipynb in exec_module(Loader, module)
        203     "        def exec_module(Loader, module):\n",
        204     "            module.__doc__ = docify(reads(Loader.get_source(Loader.name), 4))\n",
    --> 205     "            return super().exec_module(module)\n",
        206     "        def source_to_code(Loader, data, path):\n",
        207     "            with __import__('io').BytesIO(data) as stream:\n",


    ~/Untitled85.ipynb in <module>()
        133     "        !jupyter nbconvert --to markdown --stdout Untitled85.ipynb > importing.md\n",
        134     "    else: \n",
    --> 135     "        assert False, \"\"\"This error only happens when the document is imported.\"\"\""
        136    ]
        137   },


    AssertionError: This error only happens when the document is imported.


## `__name__ == '__main__'` convention

Developing notebooks as modules is like writing Python backwards.  An interactive notebook begins with `__name__ == '__main__'`, when imports maintain `__name__ == __file__.rstrip('.ipynb')`.  This flag is used frequently to support different statements and expressions in interactive and module modes respectively.


```python
    if __name__ == '__main__':
        !jupyter nbconvert --to markdown --stdout Untitled85.ipynb > importing.md
    else: 
        assert False, """This error only happens when the document is imported."""
```

issue_title: Switch from scrypt to cryptography's Scrypt back-end
issue_body:
In the upcoming release, we use `scrypt` for key-lengthening in the wallet implementation.  We might be able to get away with using the `scrypt` backend in the cryptography.io package ([docs](https://cryptography.io/en/latest/hazmat/backends/interfaces/#cryptography.hazmat.backends.interfaces.ScryptBackend.derive_scrypt)).  This would be nice since it cuts down our dependency graph and it makes the Mac OS X build easier to come by.

Thoughts @kantai?

issue_title: Unresolveable I202
issue_body:
Hi there,

I upgraded `flake8-import-order` for [flake8](https://gitlab.com/pycqa/flake) and it started failing on L22 of our `src/flake8/__init__.py` file. I couldn't figure out how to resolve it so I simply had to silence it. Below are the contents of the file:

```py
"""Top-level module for Flake8.

This module

- initializes logging for the command-line tool
- tracks the version of the package
- provides a way to configure logging for the command-line tool

.. autofunction:: flake8.configure_logging

"""
import logging
try:
    from logging import NullHandler
except ImportError:
    class NullHandler(logging.Handler):
        """Shim for version of Python < 2.7."""

        def emit(self, record):
            """Do nothing."""
            pass
import sys  # noqa: I202

LOG = logging.getLogger(__name__)
LOG.addHandler(NullHandler())

# Clean up after LOG config
del NullHandler

__version__ = '3.5.0'
__version_info__ = tuple(int(i) for i in __version__.split('.') if i.isdigit())


# There is nothing lower than logging.DEBUG (10) in the logging library,
# but we want an extra level to avoid being too verbose when using -vv.
_EXTRA_VERBOSE = 5
logging.addLevelName(_EXTRA_VERBOSE, 'VERBOSE')

_VERBOSITY_TO_LOG_LEVEL = {
    # output more than warnings but not debugging info
    1: logging.INFO,  # INFO is a numerical level of 20
    # output debugging information
    2: logging.DEBUG,  # DEBUG is a numerical level of 10
    # output extra verbose debugging information
    3: _EXTRA_VERBOSE,
}

LOG_FORMAT = ('%(name)-25s %(processName)-11s %(relativeCreated)6d '
              '%(levelname)-8s %(message)s')


def configure_logging(verbosity, filename=None, logformat=LOG_FORMAT):
    """Configure logging for flake8.

    :param int verbosity:
        How verbose to be in logging information.
    :param str filename:
        Name of the file to append log information to.
        If ``None`` this will log to ``sys.stderr``.
        If the name is "stdout" or "stderr" this will log to the appropriate
        stream.
    """
    if verbosity <= 0:
        return
    if verbosity > 3:
        verbosity = 3

    log_level = _VERBOSITY_TO_LOG_LEVEL[verbosity]

    if not filename or filename in ('stderr', 'stdout'):
        fileobj = getattr(sys, filename or 'stderr')
        handler_cls = logging.StreamHandler
    else:
        fileobj = filename
        handler_cls = logging.FileHandler

    handler = handler_cls(fileobj)
    handler.setFormatter(logging.Formatter(logformat))
    LOG.addHandler(handler)
    LOG.setLevel(log_level)
    LOG.debug('Added a %s logging handler to logger root at %s',
              filename, __name__)
```

issue_title: Impolite service termination
issue_body: In the finalizer for the plugin, the [current termination logic](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-services/blob/b55325985496b7a0fec13f9bf55e12915c83215e/pytest_services/service.py#L72-L79) is to first kill, then terminate the proc. On Windows, these two calls are the same, but on Unix, these calls should be reversed. `terminate` is the more polite operation, asking the child to shutdown cleanly, whereas `kill` is equivalent to `kill -9` and will cause the child to simply stop without cleaning up. This approach means that a complex service that starts its own child services will stop but leave these other processes running.

issue_title: Rank Nginx vhosts by whether they are SSL-enabled
issue_body:
(Slightly related to #2059)

One technique for managing Nginx vhosts is to have two config files per server name, one for HTTP and one for HTTPS. Both files would contain the same server_name, and the Nginx configurator would pick one arbitrarily to install the certificate in.

Instead, configurator.py's `_get_ranked_matches` should use the SSL status of a vhost as part of its ranking, and preferentially install certificates in a vhost which already has SSL enabled.

  "log.connection.close": False,
  "log_level": 3,
  "log.queue": False
})
kafka_consumer.subscribe([master_config["kafka"]["topic"]])

running = True
while running:
  message_object = kafka_consumer.poll()
  if message_object and not message_object.error():
    print('Received message: %s' % message_object.value().decode('utf-8'))
    ... do processing
  elif message_object and message_object.error().code() != KafkaError._PARTITION_EOF:
      print(message_object.error())
      running = False
  else:
    print("Got None for message_object!")
```

How to reproduce
================

Setup an Aiven Kafka cluster. Run the previous Kafka code. Watch messages disappear sometimes.

=========
Please provide the following information:

 - [*] confluent-kafka-python and librdkafka version (`confluent_kafka.version()` and `confluent_kafka.libversion()`): ('0.11.0', 720896) and ('0.11.0', 721151)

 - [*] Client configuration: `{

  "security.protocol": 'ssl',
  "ssl.key.location": master_config["kafka"]["service.key"],
  "ssl.certificate.location": master_config["kafka"]["service.cert"],
  "ssl.ca.location": master_config["kafka"]["ca.pem"],
  "group.id": 0,
  "log.connection.close": False,
  "log_level": 3,
  "log.queue": False
}`

 - [ ] Provide client logs (with `'debug': '..'` as necessary)

issue_title: Possibility to have a strict dayfirst
issue_body:
pandas uses the `dateutil.parser.parse` functions in its [`to_datetime`](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/generated/pandas.to_datetime.html) method (and it's what makes this a powerful method to be able to parse all kind of sorts datetime strings!)

But the pandas `to_datetime` function can parse lists (or arrays) of strings at once, and a regular bug report we get is that you can end up with inconsistent parsing within that list (because of the flexibility of dateutil). 
For example:

```
In [20]: pd.to_datetime(["31/12/2014", "10/03/2011"])
Out[20]: DatetimeIndex(['2014-12-31', '2011-10-03'])
```

So in the above, both strings are parsed in another way. Of course, in this case it can be solved by passing `dayfirst=True` (which we pass through to `dateutil.parser.parse`). But say you have a malformed string:

```
In [21]: pd.to_datetime(["31/12/2014", "10/13/2011"], dayfirst=True)
Out[21]: DatetimeIndex(['2014-12-31', '2011-10-13'])
```

Regardless of the `dayfirst=True` that is passed, not all strings are parsed with using day first, but you end up with a mixture of using dayfirst or not depending on the string.

So our **question is if it would be possible to be able to specify that dayfirst/yearfirst have to be strict** (for example with an extra keyword), so that if the provided string cannot be parsed following the given dayfirst/yearfirst argument, that an error is raised instead of parsing the string anyway without following dayfirst/yearfirst.

The original issue is here: https://github.com/pydata/pandas/issues/3341

issue_title: Powerline for Zsh not connecting to daemon
issue_body:
My powerline prompt in Zsh does not seem to be connecting to the daemon.

```
% cat /etc/lsb-release
DISTRIB_ID=Ubuntu
DISTRIB_RELEASE=16.04
DISTRIB_CODENAME=xenial
DISTRIB_DESCRIPTION="Ubuntu 16.04.1 LTS"
```

```
% zsh --version
zsh 5.1.1 (x86_64-ubuntu-linux-gnu)
```

```
% pip show powerline-status | grep '^Version'
Version: 2.5
```

```
% file ~/.local/bin/powerline
/home/xandor/.local/bin/powerline: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, x86-64, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, interpreter /lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2, for GNU/Linux 2.6.32, BuildID[sha1]=ae4f0504ed8a97f976490efd936f45a11b4fd61a, not stripped
```

`powerline-daemon` is started by Tmux (which is run before Zsh); `.tmux.conf`:
```
PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"
run-shell "powerline-daemon -q"
run-shell 'tmux source-file "$(python -m site --user-site)/powerline/bindings/tmux/powerline.conf"'
```

Relevant part of `.zshrc`:
```
PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"
source "$(python -m site --user-site)/powerline/bindings/zsh/powerline.zsh"
```

(`PATH` is exported later in `.zshrc`. Incidentally I'm actually adding `$HOME/.local/bin` to `PATH` only once because the setting in `.tmux.conf` is necessary for Powerline to work for Tmux, but it doesn't export to the shell env, which is exactly the behaviour I want).

`~/.config/powerline/config.json`:
```
{
	"common": {
		"term_truecolor": false
	},
	"ext": {
		"shell": {
			"colorscheme": "solarized",
			"theme": "default",
			"local_themes": {
				"continuation": "continuation",
				"select": "select"
			}
		},
		"tmux": {
			"colorscheme": "solarized",
			"theme": "default"
		}
	}
}
```

I'm using `libzpython`. Contrary to the README of zpython, debian-based OSs do have the correct headers in the `zsh-dev` package to be able to build. I built it against Python 2.7 (using `cmake .. -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=/usr/local -DPYTHON_INCLUDE_DIR=/usr/include/python2.7 -DPYTHON_LIBRARY=/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libpython2.7.so && make && sudo make install`).

I've been struggling with the performance of Powerline for Zsh on my Ubuntu box for a little while, wondering what the problem was. I edited `~/.local/bin/powerline-daemon` to print some statements in the `do_run` function. When I then ran `powerline-daemon --foreground` (after killing it first), there is no output from the shell client. Tmux client was definitely connected, lots of activity indicated by my added print statements. When I removed the powerline bindings from `.tmux.conf` and again looked for activity from the shell, there was simply no output from `powerline-daemon`, indicating no connections from any clients.

I'm not using Powerline for any other software at the moment.

Please advise :-).

issue_title: NDArray: Empty ndarrays become array scalars (of dangerously random value!)
issue_body:
To set this up, when I say an "array scalar", I only mean a numpy array with shape `()`:

```python
>>> np.array(3)
array(3)
>>> np.array(3).shape
()
```
This is subtly different (but behaves mostly identically) from an array of shape `(1,)`:

```python
>>> np.array([3])
array([3])
>>> np.array([3]).shape
(1,)
```
First, I want to point out that array scalars are automatically converted to `(1,)`, which I think is an acceptable (but perhaps not ideal) behavior if it makes sense for the SArray:

```python
>>> tc.SArray([np.array(3), np.array([3]), np.array([[3]])])
dtype: ndarray
Rows: 3
[array([3.]), array([3.]), array([[3.]])]
```
However, this issue is really about empty arrays. Empty arrays can have any shape as long as at least one axis has size 0. However, as they go into an SArray, for some reason they get converted to array scalars:

```python
>>> xx = [np.array([]), np.array([[]]), np.ones((3, 3, 10))[:, :0].copy()]
>>> [x.shape for x in xx]
[(0,), (1, 0), (3, 0, 10)]
>>> tc.SArray(x)
dtype: ndarray
Rows: 3
[array(0.), array(0.), array(0.)]
>>> tc.SArray(x).apply(np.shape)
dtype: list
Rows: 3
[[], [], []]
```
(note, the `copy()` is just to avoid triggering #355)

But wait, where did it get 0 from? Is it always 0? No, it's in fact peeking at some unrelated memory:

```python
>>> tc.SArray([np.array([4])])
dtype: ndarray
Rows: 1
[array([4.])]
>>> tc.SArray([np.array([])])
dtype: ndarray
Rows: 1
[array(4.)]
>>> tc.SArray([np.array([5])])
dtype: ndarray
Rows: 1
[array([5.])]
>>> tc.SArray([np.array([])])
dtype: ndarray
Rows: 1
[array(5.)]
```

issue_title: Improve performance of solarposition.ephemeris
issue_body:
`solarposition.ephemeris` returns results in dataframe with a localized time index, rather than UTC time. This results in slow performance because the dataframe is assembled from Series and np.arrays that are calculated using UTC time, requiring a timezone conversion.

**To Reproduce**
Change line 693 from
`DFOut = pd.DataFrame(index=time)`

to 

`DFOut = pd.DataFrame(index=time_utc)`

to observe the speedup: roughly 23 sec calculation time for 500k timesteps with `index=time`, and <5 seconds for `index=time_utc`

 - ``pvlib.__version__``: 0.5.2
 - ``pandas.__version__``:  0.20.3

Recommend that `solarposition.ephemeris` return a dataframe indexed in UTC time.

issue_title: Threading issue with new SQL Observer
issue_body:
Sacred uses threading.Timer objects to execute heartbeat events to update the experiment's database records with the latest buffered IO while the experiment is running.  However, the libraries used by SQL Observer are not thread safe, and this is causing long-running experiments to randomly crash for me.

The exact error differs from run to run.  In some cases, a transaction is created in one thread, and then used on another, which the library will not allow.  In other cases, it tries to use a transaction that has already been rolled back.  In still other cases, experiments run without issue, but the longer-running experiments (10+ minutes) tend to crash more often.

I've temporarily worked around this issue by disabling heartbeat events altogether (by directly editing the Sacred 0.70 library code on the install of my machine).  Once I did that, the library works as intended.

I'm using the SQL Observer with an SQLite3 database on a Windows 10 machine.  I have not tested this on my Mac or Ubuntu machines, but I will update the ticket if I manage to duplicate the issue on those systems as well.

issue_title: senza patch: allow updating launch configurations and auto scaling groups
issue_body:
I propose to add a new Senza command "patch".
The "patch" command should be able to update existing Cloud Formation resources (without using Cloud Formation "update" as this might not be feasible because of DNS traffic switching):

* update launch configurations to use latest Taupage AMI
* update launch configurations with changed Taupage config (different Docker image, environment variables, etc)
* rollout changes for all ASG instances (similar to https://github.com/ImmobilienScout24/aws-ha-updater)

The command line interface could look like:

```bash
# "patch" allows using an existing Senza YAML
# update launch configuration according to current Senza YAML 
# and respawn instances ("apply"):
$ senza patch myapp.yaml 1 --apply  
# or allows patching individual properties (pre-defined patches)
# use latest Taupage AMI and terminate/respawn instances:
$ senza patch mystack 1 --latest-taupage-image --apply

issue_title: sorts inhabited by default?
issue_body:
Hi all,

on the VC below Z3 answers "unsat" although one could argue that it's not ... the critical question is whether sorts are always inhabited or not. Clearly Z3 assumes that yes, but the SMTlib standard doesn't say anything about this as far as I can see. And other SMT-solvers (I tried CVC4 and Alt-ergo) don't exploit this.

So what is your opinion on this? Thanks in advance,

Johannes

================ VC below ===================

(declare-sort t 0)

(define-fun in_range ((x Int)) Bool (and (<= 1 x) (<= x 0)))

(declare-fun to_rep (t) Int)

;; range_axiom
  (assert (forall ((x t)) (in_range (to_rep x))))

(check-sat)

issue_title: Typing (sort-ing) error - bug or expected?
issue_body:
Z3 4.5.1 nightly (1f29cebd4df6) rejects the following program with `unknown constant bar`:

```text
(declare-sort S1)
(declare-sort S2)
(declare-sort S3)

(declare-fun foo (Bool) S1)
(declare-fun foo (Bool) S2)

(declare-fun bar (S2 S2) Bool)
(declare-fun bar (S3 S3) Bool)

(declare-const s2 S2)
(declare-const b Bool)

(assert (bar s2 (foo b))) ;;; ERROR
```

I can see that the overloaded `foo` can give rise to type/sort ambiguities in general, but in this example, I would expect that, in the final `assert`, the type system concludes that `bar: S2 ⨯ S2 → Bool` and `foo: Bool → S2` are to be applied. Is Z3's behaviour expected or is this a bug in the type-checker?

I tried working around the apparent ambiguity using SMT2's `as` terms, but didn't succeed: it seems that only constants can be sort-annotated via `as`. Is there another solution/work-around other than renaming `foo` (e.g. to `fooS1` and `fooS2`)?

issue_title: Bitvector Division by Zero is Possibly Overconstrained
issue_body:
On the `unstable` branch at commit 424f34d3d948283b61c92f089cc3b90e76c44324, if I run Z3 with the following command-line:

```console
z3 rewriter.hi_div0=false old_simplify.bv.hi_div0=false test.smt2
```

...where `test.smt2` has the following contents:

```
(declare-const x (_ BitVec 1))
(assert (= x (bvudiv #b0 #b0)))
(assert (= x #b0))
(check-sat)
```

...Z3 reports `unknown`.  My interpretation of the standard is that it should be `sat` in this instance, as the result of bitvector division by zero is unconstrained, which is consistent with the behavior for reals and integers.  For what it's worth, CVC4 reports `sat` for this input.

I realize this may be more of a discussion point than a bug report, based on [some prior discussion](http://sourceforge.net/p/smtcomp/mailman/message/32457410/).  I'm honestly not sure what the correct behavior is here.

issue_title: Strings: z3str3 str.substr Floating Point Exception / Segmentation Fault
issue_body:
I am not sure if I should report z3str3 issues here, and I apologize if this is not the right place.

On the following example using smt.string_solver=z3str3 I get a segmentation fault
```
(set-logic ALL)
(set-option :produce-models true)
(declare-const s1 String)
(declare-const index Int)
(assert (= (str.len s1) 5))
(assert (= index 2))
(assert (= ( str.++ ( str.substr s1 0 index)
                    ( str.substr s1 (+ index 1) (str.len s1)) ) s1))
(check-sat)
```

On this modified version I get Floating Point Exception
```
(set-logic ALL)
(set-option :produce-models true)
(declare-const s1 String)
(assert (= (str.len s1) 5))
(assert (= ( str.++ ( str.substr s1 0 4)
                    ( str.substr s1 5 5) ) s1))
(check-sat)
```

Z3 without z3str3 solves this correctly (UNSAT).

issue_title: improve initialization of non-public PostgreSQL schemas during db init
issue_body:
Randy Syring [4:52 PM] 
> Wierd... `climate develop db init --clear-first` creates the stage schema... `climate develop db init` doesn't....

[4:52] 
@tonto: yes, its is a bit unintuative

[4:54] 
The reason is that the code to create the schemas only gets called for the "prep_empty" event: https://github.com/level12/keg/blob/master/keg/db/dialect_ops.py#L65
 
GitHub
level12/keg
keg - Keg: more than Flask

Nick Zaccardi [4:55 PM] 
I see that.

Randy Syring [4:55 PM] 
Which only gets called on `db_clear()`: https://github.com/level12/keg/blob/master/keg/db/__init__.py#L109
 
GitHub
level12/keg
keg - Keg: more than Flask

Nick Zaccardi [4:55 PM] 
It should probably get called both, times, yea?

Randy Syring [4:56 PM] 
The logic could be cleaned up, yes.  But, it's not that simple.

[4:58] 
My assumption has always been that the DB starts off ready to create objects as needed, i.e. the database and schemas are already in place.  So, it's kind of due to the way that we clear the PostgreSQL schemas of their objects (by dropping the schemas) that we even need to worry about this.

[4:58] 
That is why it's called `prep_empty()` and tied to the clear step.

Nick Zaccardi [4:58 PM] 
What about when we first create the database?

[4:59] 
I suppose my point is, what is the correct way to create a brand new environment?

Randy Syring [4:59 PM] 
in the past, i've always assumed that initial creation of the DB (by the developer) would leave the DB in such a state that all objects could be created by the application.

[4:59] 
Alembic?

[5:00] 
default DB state + Alembic run should make it ready?

Nick Zaccardi [5:00 PM] 
When I create the database it doesn't create all the schemas, it only creates one.

Randy Syring [5:00 PM] 
yes, my assumption breaks down in those environments.

Nick Zaccardi [5:00 PM] 
So what I am hearing you say is, the provision step should create it.

[5:01] 
That is fine, I was falsely under the impression that `db init` did that.

Randy Syring [5:01 PM] 
That seems reasonable to me...but I could also see adjusting Keg to be smart about creating schemas that don't exist that it knows it needs.

Nick Zaccardi [5:01 PM] 
Right.

Randy Syring [5:01 PM] 
In fact, that would probably be better...I'm only explaining why it works the way it works now, not saying that is the best way.

issue_title: RFC: Unify conv/pool layers
issue_body:
It has come up a couple of times as I have introduced people (cc @bmcfee, @moscow25, and issue #128) to Lasagne that someone is trying to use some code which uses, for example, `Conv2DDNNLayer` on a machine which has no GPU or does not have cudnn installed (this can happen when you're running someone else's code, or when you want to test out some code you wrote on your GPU box on your GPU-less laptop).  The simple fix is generally to just use `Conv2DLayer` instead, but it's not really a portable solution to tell people to modify code when it's being used on different systems.  A simple hack fix would be for all scripts which use Lasagne's layers which have optional dependencies to have something like this (as an example) at the top:
```Python
try:
    import lasagne.layers.dnn
    conv_layer = lasagne.layers.dnn.Conv2DNNLayer
except ImportError:
    conv_layer = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer
...
# All convolutional layers would then be instantiated like this
layer = conv_layer(incoming, ...)
```
I don't really think it's fair to encourage all of our users to do something this hacky, though, and I think we owe it to users to abstract this out.  To me, a good solution would be for there to be a single `Conv2DLayer` which had an additional kwarg such as `backend`, which could be, say, `'theano'`, `'cuda_convnet'`, `'dnn'`, etc., which chose which backend to use.  Given that the different backends have different requirements, we'd need to be diligent (as we already are) about throwing errors when a backend can't support the provided layer arguments.  The simplest way to implement this would be to basically just make `Conv2DLayer` a wrapper class around all of these layers, which threw warnings when it fell back on the theano backend because of something not being present.  I believe this also applies to pooling layers.  If and when theano does this for us, we can greatly simplify our code, but I think it's worth abstracting this out now instead of waiting.  I don't have enough in-depth knowledge of the different layers/backends to know if there is something making this not possible, so please let me know if there is.

issue_title: "nbconvert"ed interactive Vega plots
issue_body:
Using the `nbconvert` tool, it would be nice be able to export notebooks containing Vega plots in html (and rst) format. For static plots it just works, but for good reasons all the interactive features of a plot disappear. Links to the `d3` and `vega` javascript libraries need to be added to the HTML template that is passed to `nbconvert`, and some magic to preserve the plot specification also need to happen in the preprocessing and conversion steps. 

Are you able to offer advice on how to write such magic? By calling the private functions `_generate_html` and `_generate_js` on the `vega.vega.Vega` object I can get the relevant snippets of code, but my knowledge about Jupyter and nbconvert is not deep enough to understand how I can make them survive through to the output HTML.

issue_title: Update pgcli tests to target mssql-cli
User's default database not followed
issue_body:
When logging in with a SQL login, the use gets dropped in to 'master' instead of the default db specified in the user's profile.

I did verify the use had a custom default setting by querying sys.server_principals & the default choice is definitely set.

No doubt there is a command-line switch that can let me set my preference, but could you use the user's default db setting when no switch is provided?

issue_title: certain notebooks don't work at all
issue_body:
when executing nbval on one of my notebooks, I get weird py.test --nbval reportings like the following:

```
<<<<<<<<<<<< Reference output from ipynb file:
stream
============ disagrees with newly computed (test) output:  
streamstreamstreamstreamstreamstreamstreamstreamstreamstreamstreamstreamstreamstreamstream
>>>>>>>>>>>>
```
```

stream
============ disagrees with newly computed (test) output:  
streamstreamstream
>>>>>>>>>>>>

stream
============ disagrees with newly computed (test) output:  
streamst...<snip base64, md5=1d90ad9225fdb1ef...>
>>>>>>>>>>>>
```

I was not able to make a minimal working example out of it yet, however maybe someone has seen this before and can help me.

[Interestingly, when downloading the example documentation.ipynb from nbval repository, everything works perfect]

issue_body: Only the "binary" wheel for Python 2 was uploaded to PyPI, making v0.8.2 uninstallable on Python3.

issue_title: Implement in-vivo symbolic execution
issue_body:
```One idea we've bounced around is to support a concretization layer like what is proposed in "In-vivo Symbolic Execution". Basically, past some point on the callstack (i.e., inside the heap functions), any symbolic data they touch is automatically concretized (with the resulting possibility of then executing the functions in unicorn). For stuff like the heap, where you usually aren't expecting symbolic data to be reasoned over, it would provide a very nice speedup, and allow us to more faithfully mirror actual execution.
```

issue_title: fails to find "../src/google/protobuf/unittest.proto" during pip install
issue_body:
During installation on Intel Edison,  it is failing during the pip install phase because it is not finding "../src/google/protobuf/unittest.proto" 

This causes a 'Failed to load module: No module named google.protobuf.internal' error when trying to load the droneapi module in mavproxy.

The error is caused by ['protobuf >= 2.5.0' ],which pulls the pip protobuf and still has line 73 as:
 
    def GenerateUnittestProtos():
      generate_proto("../src/google/protobuf/unittest.proto")

The official git has updated setup.py to include:
    
    def GenerateUnittestProtos():
        # Unittest protos are only needed for development.
        if not os.path.exists("../.git"):
        return
    generate_proto("../src/google/protobuf/unittest.proto")

This problem can be fixed by editing the protobuf/setup.py file and re-running pip install.

issue_title: The request() function just use the old version of Graph API
issue_body:
I suspect an issue in request()
```
 response = requests.request(method or "GET",
                                        FACEBOOK_GRAPH_URL + path,
                                        timeout=self.timeout,
                                        params=args,
                                        data=post_args,
                                        proxies=self.proxies,
                                        files=files)
```
I think this function should use the version field, which one is defined as the parameter self.version. Otherwise, this function will use the old version number to request data from facebook server. So I advise modify it like this:
`response = requests.request(method or "GET",
                                        FACEBOOK_GRAPH_URL + self.version + "/" + path,
                                        timeout=self.timeout,
                                        params=args,
                                        data=post_args,
                                        proxies=self.proxies,
                                        files=files)`

issue_title: OpenID payload cache uses the wrong cache key
issue_body:
The `cache_key` becomes the same for every access token due to this bug: https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/blob/e1e0d6be0024418fd100210901f9d2ca06344fe1/kinto/plugins/openid/__init__.py#L51
No matter what the `hmac_tokens` variable is the `cache_key` always becomes `'openid:verify:%s'`.

[metadata]
name = pifpaf

[wheel]

issue_body:
When I go to change password page, it doesn't show proper password warnings well because of <ul> tags.

Default Django theme uses  " | safe " to solve this.
In django-jet, on templates/registration/password_change_form.html line 36 and 44, you should put "**| safe**" after "**help_text**" to solve this problem.

issue_title: Manage livy endpoint from magics
issue_body:
This will be the API:

* `%spark add session_name language conn_string`
  will create a session against the endpoint specified
* `%spark info`
  will display the info for the sessions created in that notebook
* `%spark config <configuration_overrides>`
  will add session configs for subsequent sessions
* `%spark info conn_string`
  will list the sessions for a given livy endpoint by providing `session_id, language, state`
* `%spark delete session_name`
  will delete a session by its name from the notebook that created it
* `%spark delete conn_string session_id`
  will delete a session for a given endpoint by its id
* `%spark cleanup`
  will delete all sessions created by the notebook
* `%spark cleanup conn_string`
  will delete all session for the given livy endpoint

This covers #56, #75, and #76 for magics in Python kernel.
We are not designing the API for the wrapper kernels here and we'll tackle that as a separate improvement.

ping @msftristew @ellisonbg to take a look when they can

issue_title: `Int` -> `Float` type coercion issue
issue_body:
There seems to be a bug in type coercion system where an `Int` is not coerced into a `Float`. Here's the code sample that should illustrate the issue:

```python3
from graphql import (
    graphql,
    GraphQLSchema,
    GraphQLObjectType,
    GraphQLField,
    GraphQLArgument,
    GraphQLString,
    GraphQLFloat,
    GraphQLInt,
)

schema = GraphQLSchema(
  query= GraphQLObjectType(
    name='RootQueryType',
    fields={
      'hello': GraphQLField(
        type= GraphQLString,
        args={
          'foo': GraphQLArgument(GraphQLFloat)
        },
        resolver=lambda *_, foo: str(foo)
      )
    }
  ),
  types=[GraphQLInt]
)

# here the coercion of Int -> Float fails
query1 = '''
    query Bad($foo: Int) {
        hello(foo: $foo)
    }
'''

result = graphql(schema, query1, variable_values={'foo': 3})
print(result.data)
print(result.errors)

# here the coercion of Int -> Float works just fine
query2 = '''
    query Good {
        hello(foo: 3)
    }
'''

result = graphql(schema, query2)
print(result.data)
print(result.errors)
```

As far as I can tell from the [spec](http://facebook.github.io/graphql/October2016/#sec-Float), this should fall under input coercion for `Float`.

I'm using version 2.0 of this package.

issue_title: Add $save and $recall metafunctions for keeping function call results for later
system_requirements() and docker doesn't play well
issue_body:
conan package tools has a feature to run builds inside docker.
note that docker invocation always mounts conan cache folder, see https://github.com/conan-io/conan-package-tools/blob/e4be5af6d8a5fb34f3ffc7273166e44bf8b97ba2/conan/create_runner.py#L161
if recipe is using system_requirements() that causes problems.
e.g. first build runs in Docker container, and installs system requirements, leaving record in cache.
then second build runs in the fresh Docker container (which obviously has no dependencies installed), but conan thinks that requirements are installed, because of leftover in cache from the previous run.
/cc @lasote @memsharded

issue_title: CONAN_DOCKER_USE_SUDO is used to start container
issue_body:
When I've run build in docker, script prompted for password for sudo.
That looks strange because my user can execute docker commands (like `docker run …`) without sudo.
I've looked into code and found that in `DockerTestPackageRunner.run` variable `self.sudo_command` is used 
According to docs `CONAN_DOCKER_USE_SUDO` env variable setted to `False` must disable running conan with sudo, but it does the opposite: it disables running of container with sudo but commands inside docker container don't depend on this variable.

issue_title: Document plugins.
issue_body:
Several important plugins today are undocumented. Each plugin should have a `README.md` page that contains

- An overview (the problem it solves + screenshots)
- Instructions on how to log information + sample code
- A section on features in the UI
- An enumeration of the HTTP routes (endpoints) that the plugin serves

issue_title: Line charts become degenerate when runs are changed in other dashboards.
issue_body:
Visit the scalars dashboard. Navigate to the image dashboard, and toggle any run. Navigate back to the scalars dashboard. Note the squished line charts.

![6y1wqoxhkvs](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4221553/30679042-61a55b70-9e4b-11e7-8cbd-e02477f4f526.png)

Part of why this happens is that TensorBoard includes all active dashboards in the DOM. All are hidden except for the selected dashboard.
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/c52bd79c7d99a306cadb5c203810cb10f3660c42/tensorboard/components/tf_tensorboard/tf-tensorboard.html#L202

Hence, the `attach` handler for each dashboard is not a proxy for whether the dashboard is selected. In general, there is no systematic way for TensorBoard to inform each dashboard of whether it is currently selected.

issue_title: Importing tflearn is slow due to tf.contrib imports
issue_body:
Hey 👋 

[Someone over at Stack Overflow was wondering](https://stackoverflow.com/q/51061689/51685) about the time it takes to import `tflearn` compared to other modules (4.3 seconds for `tflearn`, 1.1 seconds for `tensorflow` in my example container).
In my SO answer I sleuthed around a bit, and it looks like the brunt of the import time comes from [`tflearn.variables` importing `tensorflow.contrib` for the `add_arg_scope` decorator](https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/8aa8436a641f019983a4d5ea2290ba98eed8a181/tflearn/variables.py#L7).

Since the `arg_scope` module isn't too complex, maybe it would be worth it to vendor it in `tflearn` to avoid the added import time?

Other places within tflearn that import `tf.contrib`, as of 8aa8436a641f019983, seem to be:

```
$ git grep tensorflow.contrib tflearn/
tflearn/data_utils.py:    from tensorflow.contrib.learn.python.learn.preprocessing.text import \
tflearn/estimators/cluster/kmeans.py:from tensorflow.contrib.factorization.python.ops import clustering_ops as c_ops
tflearn/estimators/cluster/kmeans.py:from tensorflow.contrib.tensor_forest.python.ops import data_ops
tflearn/estimators/ensemble/forest.py:from tensorflow.contrib.tensor_forest.python import tensor_forest
tflearn/estimators/ensemble/forest.py:from tensorflow.contrib.tensor_forest.python.ops import data_ops
tflearn/initializations.py:    from tensorflow.contrib.layers.python.layers.initializers import \
tflearn/initializations.py:    from tensorflow.contrib.layers.python.layers.initializers import \
tflearn/layers/recurrent.py:    from tensorflow.contrib.rnn.python.ops.core_rnn import static_rnn as _rnn, static_bidirectional_rnn as _brnn
tflearn/layers/recurrent.py:    from tensorflow.contrib.rnn.python.ops import core_rnn_cell
tflearn/variables.py:from tensorflow.contrib.framework.python.ops import add_arg_scope as contrib_add_arg_scope
```

Of these, it looks like `tflearn.variables`, `tflearn.data_utils`, `tflearn.initializations` and `tflearn.layers.recurrent` are always imported, so it might be worth it to look into lazily importing the `tf.contrib` dependencies in them?

issue_title: "Null" Class & Approximate Tokens
issue_body:
Hi,

2 quick questions that perhaps run the risk of  being more general than just specific fastText - but relating to the classification capability:

1) Given a corpus of short messages (something like Tweets), how (could?) one approach a problem where the vast majority of documents are of a "null class" while a minority are of a specific labelled class. It's possible to include in the training set of "null class" examples, however, the null class has a much wider array of possible tokens (including many unobserved tokens) 

Is there some way to get the pseudo-probability (rather than k most likely classes) from the predict function? Then if a given document did not reach some threshold, it could be considered as not strongly matching any labelled class. 

2) Does fastText support any concept rather than exact tokens? The use case here is spelling error (ie. we may have observed "AMAZON" but not "AMZON" despite them having presumably the same meaning). 


Thanks,
Alex

issue_title: Mutable C++ lvalue arguments for immutable or rvalue Python types
issue_body:
I was wondering whether pybind11 happily accepting this kind of functions could be misleading: 

```cpp
.def("f", [](int& x) { x = 42; })
``` 

In a more complex example, one may assume  that pushing back to a `std::vector<>&` would update the list.

We essentially have rvalues that are not treated as such in C++ because they are hosted within type casters. There's a bunch of type casters (like int/float one, etc) where the resulting Python object is immutable anyway. Should we somehow discriminate them (and possibly reject such signatures), does it make sense?

To add to (my) surprise:

```cpp 
enum class Foo { A = 1, B = 2 };

// ...

py::enum_<Foo>(m, "Foo")
 .value("A", Foo::A)
 .value("B", Foo::B);

m.def("h1", [](Foo& x) { x = Foo::B; })     // will not update x
 .def("h2", [](Foo* x) { *x = Foo::B; });  // will update x
```

issue_title: Named parameters for callbacks
issue_body:
Pybind11 has worked wonders for a project I'm working on.  Thanks for the hard work!

I have been unable to determine if it is possible to have named parameters for callbacks whose implementation is in C++.  For example, `example5.py` does the following at the very end:

```
f = test_callback4()
print("func(43) = %i" % f(43))
```

Is there any way to implement named parameters on the C++ side like the following:
```
f = test_callback4()
print("func(43) = %i" % f(number=43))
```

issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
Ubuntu 16.04.1

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
`wget https://dl.eff.org/certbot-auto`

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
```
# certbot-auto renew --dry-run
Saving debug log to /var/log/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.log

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Processing /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/some.obfuscated.domain.com.conf
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Cert not due for renewal, but simulating renewal for dry run
Running pre-hook command: iptables -t nat -I PREROUTING -p tcp --dport 443 -j REDIRECT --to-port 44033
Renewing an existing certificate
Performing the following challenges:
tls-sni-01 challenge for some.obfuscated.domain.com
tls-sni-01 challenge for some.obfuscated.aliasdomain.com
Waiting for verification...
Cleaning up challenges
Generating key (2048 bits): /etc/letsencrypt/keys/0002_key-certbot.pem
Creating CSR: /etc/letsencrypt/csr/0002_csr-certbot.pem
Dry run: skipping renewal hook command: iptables-save

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
new certificate deployed without reload, fullchain is
/etc/letsencrypt/live/some.obfuscated.domain.com/fullchain.pem
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
** DRY RUN: simulating 'certbot renew' close to cert expiry
**          (The test certificates below have not been saved.)

Congratulations, all renewals succeeded. The following certs have been renewed:
  /etc/letsencrypt/live/some.obfuscated.domain.com/fullchain.pem (success)
** DRY RUN: simulating 'certbot renew' close to cert expiry
**          (The test certificates above have not been saved.)
Running post-hook command: iptables -t nat -D PREROUTING -p tcp --dport 443 -j REDIRECT --to-port 44033
```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
I defined a "global" `renew-hook` in `/etc/letsencrypt/cli.ini`, and a per-lineage `renew-hook` in `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal/$LINEAGE.conf`, and apparently it only runs the renew-hook in cli.ini and ignores renew-hook in the per-lineage conf.

I originally thought that both hooks will run, or at least the per-lineage one will override the 'global' one.

Is this by design? What if I need different post-processing for different lineages?

By not preferring per-lineage hook over the global hook, I am forced to create a script that consolidates handling of all lineages. This greatly increase complexity of a configuration management system.

issue_title: Distinguish renewer run from cron from renewer run interactively
issue_body:
The renewer will probably normally run from `/etc/cron.daily` on most systems, and there is a config file option to inhibit this from doing anything. But if the renewer is run manually, it should probably be able to perform renewals and ignore that option (thus distinguishing "automated renewal" from "manual renewal").

One option for this is just to look at `sys.argv[0]` to see what name the renewer was run under, and tell packagers to call the symlink in `/etc/cron.daily` something other than the name of the manual renewer script.

Cc @pde (technically another aspect of CLI design, maybe?).

issue_title: UPnP exception on next launch after unclean shutdown
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

In environments that use upnp for external port mapping abruptly terminating the raiden process (i.e. `kill -9`, power loss etc.) may leave an upnp port mapping behind which will cause a `ConflictInMappingEntry` exception on next launch in [`upnpsocket.py:63`](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/network/upnpsock.py#L63).

## Solution

Check if an upnp mapping exists before trying to register a new one.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Implement check for existing upnp mappings before registering a new one

issue_title: %shared magic or %sos_options --shared
issue_body:
We already have explicit data exchange using magics `%get` and `%put`, and implicit data exchange of variables with names starting with `sos`. Just for users who like to use implicit data exchange but hate the `sos` prefix, perhaps we can provide a way to allow users to specify variables to be shared? We could either make it a list of variables or make it a pattern (e.g. `sos*` to include the default case), make it a dedicated magic `%shared` or an option for `sos` (through `%sos_options`.

Implementation wise this is not very difficult but we need to refrain from providing two much features that might back-fire later.

```

Some quick research led to: http://forums.fast.ai/t/lesson-4-oserror-cant-find-model-en/11252 and I fixed it by running `$ python -m spacy download en`.

I'm wondering if we should add a note to the notebook for that lesson. Another (maybe better) solution is to install en_core_web_sm (>= 2.0.0) with the environment.yml since that is what the en language installs for me.

Just trying to remove any friction for people using the app. How would you feel about a pull request for one of those?

issue_title: Doctr configure command doesn't work locally on Windows due to lack of native ssh-keygen executable
issue_body:
I'm a contributor with the [Spyder IDE](https://github.com/spyder-ide), and we're currently completing the process of transitioning our docs to be deployed using ``doctr`` (I'll send a PR to add our name as soon as they officially are declared production-status very soon). When running ``doctr configure`` on Windows, key generation failed due to it being unable to find a ``ssh-keygen`` binary on the PATH. Therefore, it seems ``doctr`` presumes it is being run on a *nix system with this available, even though this is not indicated anywhere I could find in the documentation and unfortunately, a significant portion of developers are stuck on Windows for one reason or another. 

After figuring out the root cause of the issue, I was able to hack a quick fix by adding the appropriate directory from my ``msysgit`` install to the $PATH$ first which had it (presumably my Cygwin install would have worked too, or anything else that had that binary). However, this obviously is pretty poor Ux for a new user, if this is in fact a problem for any Windows user that doesn't have some sort of ``msys`` install or the like on their path.

Therefore, at minimum, the documentation should presumably be updated to reflect this and the fix, or perhaps a more sustainable solution could be devised (e.g. autodetecting an existing ``MSYS`` install like with GfW or Cygwin, etc. or perhaps even WSL, and if not notifying the user interactively of the problem and how to fix it. Thanks!

issue_title: support for saving datasets
issue_body: Currently torchtext doesn't seem to support saving datasets in an easy way. Pickle doesn't also seem to work due to the existence of generators and lambda functions in the Dataset / Field objects, would it be possible to provide and API for saving datasets? This is particularly helpful for loading pre-processed datasets where the pre-processing / vocabulary creation takes a long time that can be skipped by doing once and then loaded a saved pre-processed copy of the dataset.

issue_title: torchvision.utils.save_image() gives wrong shape of image.
issue_body:
I used torchvision.utils.save_image() to save a torch float tensor, however I found that the shape of saved image was 4 pixel lager than the shape of the tensor in both height and width.

I load the image with torchvision.transforms.ToTensor() and nothing else, and then directly save it into a png image with torchvision.utils.save_image(). However it's still 4 pixel lager.

issue_title: DependsOn of a component should implicitly depend on all it's children
issue_body:
Today, passing a `ComponentResource` as a `DependsOn` resource will not do anything.  Users typically try to do this as a way to say "I want to depend on everything in this component being created before creating this other resource".  We should make that the meaning of `dependsOn: [component]`.

Of course, this will be fairly coarse grained, and may involve depending on more than strictly necessary - so component authors and users may want to expose capabilities to depend on only certain critical pieces so that other work can proceed in parallel with part of the component creation.  But the right default meaning for this seems to be the conservative "depend on all my children".

issue_title: Push a message to the driver upon worker death
issue_body: When a worker dies, we provide availability by marking the task as lost and later reexecuting the task if the result is needed. However, if the worker death is a chronic problem related to the task itself, the workload will hang because every worker that tries to reexecute the task will die. For this reason, we should notify the users after (repeated) worker deaths, probably by pushing a message to the driver through Redis.

issue_title: RRTMG extra dimension handling
issue_body:
Two main points here:

- I may have found a bug, details below.
- The number of shortwave bands should be hard set by us somehow, since it is defined by a parameter in the Fortran code that can't be edited during runtime. This makes sense, if you consider that there's no way for us to configure what frequencies the shortwave bands correspond to.

In `rrtmg_sw_rad.f90`, the comments say the following:

    ! This call to RRTMG_SW must be preceeded by a call to the module
    !     mcica_subcol_gen_sw.f90 to run the McICA sub-column cloud generator,
    !     which will provide the cloud physical or cloud optical properties
    !     on the RRTMG quadrature point (ngptsw) dimension.

However, we never call mcica_subcol_gen_sw. That file produces all arrays that are meant to be of dimension `ngptsw`. Whatever we're doing is something quite weird.

For example, tracking cloud fraction, [at the component interface](https://github.com/CliMT/climt/blob/develop/climt/_components/rrtmg/sw/component.py#L424) we have `'cloud_area_fraction_in_atmosphere_layer'` which becomes `cloud_fraction` [in the cython wrapper](https://github.com/CliMT/climt/blob/develop/climt/_components/rrtmg/sw/_rrtmg_sw.pyx#L121), of dimension `[mid_levels, lat, lon]` at that point in Fortran ordering. That is [passed to](https://github.com/CliMT/climt/blob/develop/climt/_components/rrtmg/sw/_rrtmg_sw.pyx#L181) the parameter `cldfmcl` [in the Fortran code](https://github.com/CliMT/climt/blob/develop/climt/_components/rrtmg/sw/_rrtmg_sw.pyx#L121), which is [supposed to be of dimension `[ngptsw, ncol, nlay]`](https://github.com/CliMT/climt/blob/develop/climt/_components/rrtmg/sw/_rrtmg_sw.pyx#L333). We've told the Fortran code that ncol is 1 and nlay is the number of vertical levels, so we can pass a column at a time. So through this we pass in an array that doesn't even have a ngptsw dimension to the Fortran code that wants such a dimension. I feel like I'm missing something here, since that seems like the code shouldn't run at all.

I'll continue digging through this.

issue_title: Fix handling of None and "" as hostnames in trio.socket
issue_body:
It looks like the Python stdlib has a weird behavior:

* `socket.getaddrinfo(None, 0)` resolves to the wildcard address(es)
* `sock.bind((None, 0))` is an error
* `socket.getaddrinfo("", 0)` is an error
* `sock.bind(("", 0))` binds to the wildcard address

Trio is currently not quite consistent. Trio's `getaddrinfo` matches the stdlib, but for `bind` we raise an error with *both* versions, because we use `getaddrinfo` to validate the user's input, and then if it passes we hand the user's input directly to the stdlib `bind`.

We should at the least support one way of calling `bind`, and perhaps we should teach both versions to support both behaviors.

issue_title: Are the unused_tcp_port fixtures really useful for trio?
issue_body:
I'm not sure either way, so here's an issue to discuss it.

The reason why they might not be: here's a little trio test of a simple server:

```python
# Placeholder for some more interesting server
async def echo(stream):
    while True:
        data = await stream.receive_some(4096)
        if data:
            await stream.send_all(data)
        else:
            await stream.aclose()
            return

async def echo_server(port, *, task_status=trio.TASK_STATUS_IGNORED):
    await trio.serve_tcp(echo, port, task_status=task_status)

# Okay here's the test for our server
# This version is BROKEN
async def test_echo_server(unused_tcp_port):
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        nursery.start_soon(echo_server, unused_tcp_port)
        stream = await trio.open_tcp_stream("localhost", unused_tcp_port)
        await stream.send_all(b"x")
        assert await stream.receive_some(1) == b"x"
        # Test finished; shut down the server
        nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()
```

This is broken... well, in two ways, actually.

The very minor way it's broken is intrinsic to the use of `unused_tcp_port`: there's always a race condition between when `unused_tcp_port` checks to find the open port, and when we actually use it -- someone might open the port in the mean time. This is rarely a problem, but it does bug me a bit.

There's also a much more important race condition: because we spawn the server in the background, and then immediately attempt to connect to it, we might end up connecting before the server is actually started up and has bound its port. In my experience this is a very common problem with other frameworks, and often requires ugly hacks like sticking a retry loop around the connect call.

Here's a better way to write this same test:

```python
async def test_echo_server():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        # This uses nursery.start, which waits for the server to be started up
        listeners = await nursery.start(echo_server, 0)
        stream = await trio.testing.open_stream_to_socket_listener(listeners[0])
        await stream.send_all(b"x")
        assert await stream.receive_some(1) == b"x"
        # Test finished; shut down the server
        nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()
```

We changed two things here: First, we directly told our server to bind to port 0, i.e., to pick a random unused port. In the old way of doing things, this would be a problem, because we wouldn't have any way to know what port it ended up picking. And second, we used `nursery.start` instead of `nursery.start_soon`.

Using `nursery.start` fixes the big important race condition, because it doesn't return until after the background task (in this case our echo server) has fully finished initializing itself. So by the time we make a client connection, we know the server is ready to accept clients.

But `nursery.start` also fixes our problem with knowing which port was picked, because it passes back a value after the server was initialized – in this case the value comes from `trio.serve_tcp`, which passes back the `SocketListeners` that it opened. And then can use `trio.testing.open_stream_to_socket_listener to connect to one of these listeners -- it automatically introspects it to figure out what port it's using, and makes a connection.

Summing up: this way of solving our problem uses 1 less feature, while solving 2 problems, in the same number of lines of code.

So given that trio has these features, I'm wondering whether `unused_tcp_port` is something we want to include or not. It's potentially still useful in some cases, I guess, and if nothing else then people who are expecting it might be grumpy if they don't find it. But OTOH maybe including it will just encourage people to do things the old kinda-broken way. As a general rule, if there are two ways of doing something and one of them works better than the other, trio tries to only include the one that works better :-)

issue_title: Add support for GET /merge_requests call
issue_body:
## Description of the problem, including code/CLI snippet

Since GitLab 9.5 there is a `GET /merge_requests` call that returns all the merge requests the current authenticated user have access to.

This call is very useful when doing actions over all the merge requests, as is much more efficient than doing `GET /projects/:id/merge_requests` for each project.

A good place for its manager to be would be in the `mergerequests` property in the client object, the same name used in project objects.
